caveats: a: The occupation source covers all general-merchandise retailers and is not 455211-specific.
a: Large-chain AI evidence may overstate both data availability and technical capacity for LMM operators.
a: Physical automation and robotics are included only where AI-linked deployment can avoid labor within five years.
rho: Walmart and Costco operate at a scale far beyond the lens.
rho: The range assumes affordable vendor products reach smaller operators and integrate with their POS, ERP, labor, and fulfillment systems.
rho: Customer acceptance and regulatory scrutiny can delay deployment even when technology works.
e: The injected LMM firm-count input is missing, so this share cannot be converted into a defensible eligible target count.
e: NAICS is assigned to establishments and can count branches rather than independently transferable firms.
e: Small regional ethnic, liquidation, or hybrid grocery/general-merchandise formats may be classified inconsistently.
s5: No industry-specific closed-deal rate with a defensible private-company denominator was found.
s5: A site or lease sale is not necessarily a transfer of the operating company.
s5: Financial distress can lead to liquidation rather than a qualifying control transfer.
q: Retention varies between membership clubs and non-membership supercenters.
q: Private-label penetration and local competitive intensity materially affect pass-through.
q: The estimate measures discounted retention of implemented gross benefit, not merchandise gross margin.
d5: The public sales series is nominal and dominated by very large chains.
d5: Food, fuel, and general-merchandise inflation differ, making constant-quality conversion difficult.
d5: Scale leaders may gain share at the expense of any LMM operators.
o: Digital purchases can still require the same retailer, so channel shift is not automatically operator elimination.
o: Marketplace and direct-to-consumer penetration could remove the operator from some non-food categories faster than from groceries.
o: Demand-volume effects are reserved for d5 rather than double-counted here.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: High-volume merchandise sales at low posted prices, often supplemented by annual membership fees, fuel, pharmacy or optical services, advertising, fulfillment, and other ancillary income; purchases repeat frequently but merchandise sales are generally not contractual.
lens.excluded_activities: Locations owned by public or very large chains, captive distribution and service units, pure e-commerce marketplaces, non-control financing vehicles, shells, and store assets without a standalone transferable operating business.
lens.included_activities: US warehouse-club and supercenter operators in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band that repeatedly sell broad lines of merchandise, including substantial grocery assortments, to external consumers or business members.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupation shares for broader NAICS 455000 general-merchandise retail rather than a wage-weighted, task-level measure for 455211. I map portions of checkout, customer service, planning, merchandising, administration, marketing, inventory control, and management to exposed tasks while treating most stocking, picking, transport, food handling, and floor presence as physical; inclusion of department stores likely overweights consultative selling and may bias exposure upward for warehouse clubs and supercenters.
caveats: The occupation source covers all general-merchandise retailers and is not 455211-specific.
Large-chain AI evidence may overstate both data availability and technical capacity for LMM operators.
Physical automation and robotics are included only where AI-linked deployment can avoid labor within five years.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory for independent 455211 operators, separating current automation from unautomated work and measuring hours in checkout, stocking, picking, food, service, merchandising, administration, and management.
a.rationale: The broad occupation mix contains 43.63% sales occupations, 8.41% office support, and 33.00% transportation/material-moving work. The first two groups and planning within the third contain exposed workflows, but pallet handling, replenishment, food operations, and in-person exception management remain labor intensive. Walmart confirms AI investment across customer experience, associate work, supply chain, operations, management, and recruiting.

d5.bridge: The Census/FRED series measures nominal US warehouse-club and supercenter sales across all firm sizes rather than constant-price, constant-quality quantity for LMM operators. I use the rise from roughly $50-$58 billion in many 2021 months to roughly $60-$68 billion in 2025 as a directional base, discounting for inflation and large-chain share; nominal growth biases an unadjusted quantity ratio upward, while scale-driven share gains can bias its relevance to LMM firms upward as well.
caveats: The public sales series is nominal and dominated by very large chains.
Food, fuel, and general-merchandise inflation differ, making constant-quality conversion difficult.
Scale leaders may gain share at the expense of any LMM operators.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 455211 transaction counts, unit volumes, store openings and closures, and comparable sales split by operator size and membership versus non-membership format.
d5.rationale: The format combines recurring groceries and household essentials with value-oriented general merchandise, and industry sales continued to rise through 2025. Costco reported 2025 comparable sales up 6%, driven by 5% higher shopping frequency and about 1% higher average ticket, supporting durable quantity demand, though LMM operators may not share the leaders' gains.

caveats: The injected LMM firm-count input is missing, so this share cannot be converted into a defensible eligible target count.
NAICS is assigned to establishments and can count branches rather than independently transferable firms.
Small regional ethnic, liquidation, or hybrid grocery/general-merchandise formats may be classified inconsistently.
e.change_evidence: A 455211 business register linked to ultimate ownership, store count, normalized EBITDA, square footage, buying-group dependence, lease ownership, and standalone audited or quality-of-earnings financials.
e.rationale: The format depends on extreme purchasing volume, distribution efficiency, large sites, broad assortments, and substantial working capital, so most recognizable operators and locations belong to very large chains. Costco's average warehouse is about 147,000 square feet and its operating model is explicitly built on volume purchasing and rapid turnover. Only a very small share of putative LMM firms is likely to be independent, standalone, repeat-customer operators with transferable systems and supplier relationships.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Digital purchases can still require the same retailer, so channel shift is not automatically operator elimination.
Marketplace and direct-to-consumer penetration could remove the operator from some non-food categories faster than from groceries.
Demand-volume effects are reserved for d5 rather than double-counted here.
o.change_evidence: Transaction-journey data separating operator-supplied, marketplace, direct-brand, self-service, and fully automated volume, plus evidence on which party bears inventory, food-safety, return, fulfillment, and consumer-remedy responsibility.
o.rationale: Most surviving quantity still requires an accountable merchant to source and finance inventory, manage food safety and shrink, operate fulfillment and returns, maintain membership records where applicable, and stand behind the transaction. Self-checkout, online ordering, automated replenishment, and agentic shopping remove tasks but generally leave the retailer accountable.

caveats: Retention varies between membership clubs and non-membership supercenters.
Private-label penetration and local competitive intensity materially affect pass-through.
The estimate measures discounted retention of implemented gross benefit, not merchandise gross margin.
q.change_evidence: Store-level post-deployment contribution-margin cohorts that track labor savings, price gaps, promotions, member fees, wages, traffic, basket size, and competitor response for at least two years.
q.rationale: Savings are not contractually passed through on ordinary merchandise sales, but price leadership is central to this format and competitive transparency is high. Costco explicitly prioritizes competitive value over short-term price maximization and sometimes holds or cuts prices at the expense of gross margin; its 2025 gross margin was only 11.12%. Membership economics can preserve some benefit, yet a substantial share is likely reinvested in prices, service, wages, or faster fulfillment.

caveats: Walmart and Costco operate at a scale far beyond the lens.
The range assumes affordable vendor products reach smaller operators and integrate with their POS, ERP, labor, and fulfillment systems.
Customer acceptance and regulatory scrutiny can delay deployment even when technology works.
rho.change_evidence: Workflow-level results from 10-20 LMM warehouse or supercenter deployments, including implementation cost, labor hours removed or avoided, accuracy, shrink, customer satisfaction, and failed deployments over at least 24 months.
rho.rationale: Highly standardized transactions, high volumes, and recurring inventory decisions make exposed workflows implementable, and scaled operators are already investing in AI and automation. LMM operators would still face integration cost, thin technical teams, data fragmentation, shrink and safety controls, privacy obligations, and the need for human exception handling.

caveats: No industry-specific closed-deal rate with a defensible private-company denominator was found.
A site or lease sale is not necessarily a transfer of the operating company.
Financial distress can lead to liquidation rather than a qualifying control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transactions from retail M&A brokers, Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, and SBA change-of-ownership loans, hand-verified to independent 455211 going concerns and divided by an eligible-firm denominator.
s5.rationale: Among the few eligible independent firms, retirement, strategic consolidation, distress, and ordinary exits can generate control transfers, but capital intensity and supplier dependence narrow the buyer pool. Closures, asset sales, internal chain reorganizations, and transfers of individual branch properties do not qualify.

principal_driver: Durable high-frequency demand and standardized, data-rich workflows across checkout, planning, service, and supply chain.
principal_weakness: An extremely small and poorly observed pool of independently transferable LMM operators in a scale-dominated format.
